note: Patient ID: 1 Date of admission: 2025-01-12 History of present illness: patient does not speak English; communication via interpreter. Lives with spouse. Alert and oriented x3, no signs of dementia. Medication taken regularly, good adherence.

Patient ID: 2 Date of admission: 2025-01-14 History of present illness: speaks English fluently. Lives alone in an apartment, no cohabitants. Mild cognitive impairment noted, memory problems reported. Occasionally forgets to take pills.

Patient ID: 3 Date of admission: 2025-01-15 History of present illness: language barrier noted. Family provides interpretation. Lives with daughter; independent in ADLs. Cognition normal, memory intact. Non-adherent to drug regimen, skipped several doses.
Patient ID: 4 Date of admission: 2025-01-17 History of present illness: no language barrier. Resides in nursing home. Patient is forgetful, signs of dementia. Medication taken regularly.

Patient ID: 5 Date of admission: 2025-01-19 History of present illness: limited English proficiency, needs interpreter. Lives alone. Alert and oriented, no cognitive impairment. Compliant with therapy, no missed doses.

Patient ID: 6 Date of admission: 2025-01-22 History of present illness: patient only speaks Spanish; daughter translates for mother. Lives independently but alone. Memory problems reported, frequent forgetfulness. Suspected medication noncompliance.
Patient ID: 7 Date of admission: 2025-01-25 History of present illness: language: fluent English. Married, living together. No cognitive impairment, memory intact. Good adherence.

Patient ID: 8 Date of admission: 2025-01-27 History of present illness: needs interpreter for consultations. Lives with family. Mild cognitive impairment, Alzheimer's disease. Frequently misses medication.

Patient ID: 9 Date of admission: 2025-01-28 History of present illness: does not speak English. Lives alone. Memory is generally good but has bad days; no diagnosed dementia. Occasionally forgets to take pills.
Patient ID: 10 Date of admission: 2025-01-30 History of present illness: no language barrier. Lives with spouse. Alert, cognition normal. Compliant with therapy.
Patient ID: 11 Date of admission: 2025-02-01 History of present illness: communication via interpreter. Lives independently; no family at home. Cognitive decline noted. Non-adherent to drug regimen.

Patient ID: 12 Date of admission: 2025-02-03 History of present illness: language barrier. Family provides interpretation, but patient main language is Spanish. Lives alone. No signs of dementia. Good adherence.
Patient ID: 13 Date of admission: 2025-02-05 History of present illness: speaks English fluently. Resides in nursing home. Alzheimer's disease, memory problems. Medication taken regularly.
Patient ID: 14 Date of admission: 2025-02-07 History of present illness: limited English proficiency. Lives with daughter. Memory intact, no cognitive issues. Missed several doses this week.
Patient ID: 15 Date of admission: 2025-02-09 History of present illness: no language barrier. Lives alone. Patient is forgetful, mild cognitive impairment. Compliant with therapy.

Patient ID: 16 Date of admission: 2025-02-11 History of present illness: needs interpreter. Lives with spouse. Alert and oriented, cognition normal. Suspected medication noncompliance – not taking as prescribed.
Patient ID: 17 Date of admission: 2025-02-13 History of present illness: language barrier noted. Lives independently but alone. Dementia signs evident. Frequently forgets pills, non-adherent.
Patient ID: 18 Date of admission: 2025-02-15 History of present illness: no language issues. Lives with family. No cognitive impairment. Good adherence, no missed doses.

Patient ID: 19 Date of admission: 2025-02-17 History of present illness: patient does not speak English, interpreter needed. Lives alone in apartment. Cognitive decline reported, frequent forgetfulness. Adherence seems variable, occasionally skips doses.
Patient ID: 20 Date of admission: 2025-02-19 History of present illness: speaks English. Lives with spouse. No memory problems, cognition normal. Compliant.

Patient ID: 21 Date of admission: 2025-02-21 History of present illness: language barrier, uses interpreter. Lives independently, no cohabitants. Mild cognitive impairment. Non-adherence suspected due to missed refills.
Patient ID: 22 Date of admission: 2025-02-23 History of present illness: no language barrier. Lives alone. Memory intact, alert. Occasionally forgets to take pills – intentional adjustment mentioned.

Patient ID: 23 Date of admission: 2025-02-25 History of present illness: daughter translates for mother – patient primary language Spanish. Lives with daughter. Dementia diagnosis. Medication taken regularly.
Patient ID: 24 Date of admission: 2025-02-27 History of present illness: English fluent. Lives with family. No cognitive issues. Compliant.

Patient ID: 25 Date of admission: 2025-03-01 History of present illness: limited English proficiency. Lives alone, independent living. Alert, memory intact. Non-adherent to drug regimen – patient states forgets sometimes.
Patient speaks only Arabic; all communication goes through a phone interpreter. Lives in a retirement community with a spouse. No cognitive complaints. Takes all medications on time.
No language problems, English is her first language. She resides alone in a flat. Daughter mentions she often misplaces keys and forgets appointments. Pharmacy reports multiple missed refills.
Limited English – speaks mainly Ukrainian. Lives with adult son who helps translate. Cognitively intact per MMSE. Compliant with heart failure medications.

English is the patient's native language. He lives by himself in a small house. He has been getting lost in the neighbourhood lately and repeats questions. Wife (separated) states he often stops taking his warfarin when he feels well.
Interpreter needed (Mandarin). Patient moved into a nursing home last month. She is fully oriented with no memory deficits. Medication administration is supervised, so adherence is excellent.
Fluent in English. Lives alone in a senior apartment. Mild forgetfulness noted, but still manages finances. Occasionally misses evening pills but generally reliable.
Language: Russian, no English. Uses a video interpreter. Lives with daughter and grandchildren. No signs of dementia. Takes all medications as prescribed.

No language barrier. Patient is recently widowed and now lives alone. She reports episodes of confusion and has been found wandering. Family concerned she isn't taking her diabetes medication consistently.
Patient has no difficulty communicating in English. He lives in a group home with 24-hour support. Cognitive decline is documented (dementia, moderate stage). All medications are managed by staff.
Spanish-speaking only; professional interpreter used. Lives independently in her own home. No cognitive issues, alert and oriented. Adherence is good as per home care nurse.
English is primary language. Lives with common-law partner. No signs of cognitive impairment. Patient admits occasionally forgetting his morning medications – 'maybe twice a week'.

Patient only speaks Vietnamese, so appointments are conducted via phone interpreter. She lives alone in a high-rise. Short-term memory is poor, often repeats conversations. Non-adherent to antihypertensives per home BP logs.
Fluent in English. Lives with wife – they just celebrated their 50th anniversary. He is a bit forgetful according to wife, but still drives. Medication compliance is excellent.

Patient speaks Haitian Creole; uses family (daughter) for interpretation, which is sometimes unreliable. Lives in an assisted‑living facility with own room. Dementia diagnosis, needs reminders for ADLs. Facility administers all meds.
No language issues. Lives alone since divorce. Memory and cognition are fully intact. Checked pill box – multiple missed doses, patient says 'I just don't like taking so many pills'.

English is fluent. The patient lives in a basement apartment with his brother. He is known for long‑standing schizophrenia; cognition otherwise stable. He is fully adherent to his antipsychotic depot injection.

Patient speaks only Cantonese; all consults require a hospital‑provided interpreter. Lives alone. Recently noted to have word‑finding difficulties and left the stove on twice. Suspected non‑compliance with metformin.

No language barrier. Patient is a long‑distance truck driver, lives alone on the road most of the year. No cognitive issues. Reports he often skips his blood pressure pills because they make him urinate too frequently.
Polish speaker with very limited English; interpreter used. Resides in a care home. Moderate cognitive impairment (MoCA 18/30). Medications are given by the nurse – 100% compliance.

English is her mother tongue. Lives alone in a condo. Complains of memory lapses but formal testing shows no objective impairment (worried well). She is religious about taking her statin and thyroid pill.
Only speaks Farsi; daughter is present today to translate. Patient lives with daughter's family. No signs of dementia. Non‑adherent to her inhalers – technique never checked.

English is his primary language. He just moved to a studio apartment alone after a divorce. He is highly functioning, no cognitive deficits. He admits he 'sometimes forgets' his evening dose when he goes out with friends.

Patient speaks Romanian; interpreter provided by hospital. Lives alone in a senior residence. Clear cognitive decline: cannot recall recent events, disoriented to time. Non‑adherence flagged by pharmacy – gaps in warfarin refills.
Fluent English. Resides with a roommate in a shared apartment. No cognitive concerns. Fully adherent to ART for HIV – viral load undetectable.

Language barrier: Korean. Uses a professional telephone interpreter. Lives alone. Early Alzheimer's type dementia diagnosed 6 months ago. Frequently misses doses, daughter finds pills all over the house.

Patient speaks only Portuguese; all communication requires a hospital interpreter. Lives with his wife in a retirement village. Cognitive assessment normal, MMSE 29/30. Takes medications exactly as prescribed.

Fluent in English. Lives alone in a ground-floor unit since husband passed away two years ago. Reports increasing forgetfulness, missed two specialist appointments last month. Pharmacy notes irregular pickup of levothyroxine.
No English – speaks Somali. Uses a video interpreter service. Resides with extended family (sister, brother-in-law, nieces). No cognitive complaints. Fully adherent to antiretroviral regimen.

English is native language. Lives by himself in rural property. Word-finding difficulties becoming apparent, family worries about his driving. Patient stopped taking statin three months ago because he read something online.
Interpreter requested: Turkish. Patient resides in a nursing home with full-time care. Alert and oriented, no dementia. All medications administered by registered nurses – 100% compliance.

Speaks English well, though with accent. Lives alone in a city apartment. Mild cognitive impairment diagnosed last year, manages with lists and phone reminders. Occasionally misses afternoon doses but catches up next day.
Language: French only, no English. Hospital interpreter present. Lives with adult children. No cognitive issues. Takes all heart medications daily without fail.

No language barrier. Recently separated and now living alone. Has been showing early signs of dementia – getting lost on familiar routes. Wife reports he often refuses to take his diabetes medication.
English is primary language. Lives in a supported accommodation facility with shared dining. Moderate dementia documented, needs prompts for personal care. Facility gives all medications on schedule.
Only speaks Bengali; interpreter booked for all visits. Lives alone in a council flat. Oriented to time, place, person. Medication compliance reported as good by visiting district nurse.
English fluent. Lives with long-term partner. No signs of cognitive impairment. Patient admits to taking his blood pressure medication only when he 'feels stressed' – maybe twice weekly.

Patient speaks Urdu; all communication requires telephone interpreter. Lives alone since children moved abroad. Short-term memory significantly impaired, cannot remember recent conversations. Non-adherent to multiple medications per GP records.
English is his first language. Lives with wife of 40 years. Some age-related forgetfulness but no dementia. Perfect adherence to his Parkinson's medication schedule.

Language: Tagalog, limited English. Family member provides ad-hoc translation. Lives in a multi-generational household. Early cognitive decline noted by family – repeats stories often. Medications managed by daughter.

Fluent English speaker. Has lived alone since her mother died last spring. Fully alert and cognitively intact. Discontinued antidepressant abruptly because she felt better – now experiencing withdrawal.
Patient speaks Dari; professional interpreter used for all consultations. Lives independently in a one-bedroom unit. No cognitive concerns. Takes medications regularly, uses a Webster pack.
English is her mother tongue. Resides in a granny flat attached to her son's home. Mild dementia, sundowning episodes reported. Son says she often refuses evening medications.
No English – speaks Amharic. Uses hospital translation service. Lives with spouse. Cognition within normal limits. Excellent compliance with post-transplant immunosuppressants.
English is primary language. Widower, lives alone in a retirement villa. Significant cognitive decline over 18 months, now needs help with finances. Pharmacy reports sporadic pickup of insulin.
Speaks only Greek; interpreter arranged. Lives in a high-care nursing facility. Severe Alzheimer's, nonverbal. All medications crushed and administered by nursing staff.
Fluent English. Lives alone in a small townhouse. No cognitive deficits, very sharp for age 82. Self-reports excellent medication adherence, uses an alarm system.
Language barrier: Punjabi. Video interpreter used. Lives with married daughter and grandchildren. Memory is intact per GP assessment. Takes all medications with breakfast.
English speaker. Has lived alone since divorce 10 years ago. Early Alzheimer's diagnosed, still in denial. Patient admits he doesn't see the point of taking cholesterol medication.
Patient speaks Serbian; interpreter utilized. Lives in a group home for adults with disabilities. No cognitive impairment. Supervised medication administration ensures full compliance.
No language barrier. Lives with his mother who is his primary carer. Moderate intellectual disability, not dementia. Mother reports he sometimes spits out his tablets.
Only speaks Tamil; hospital provides interpreter. Lives alone in a bedsit. Diagnosed with vascular dementia after a stroke. Missed several warfarin doses, INR subtherapeutic.
English first language. Lives with his wife in a retirement community. Mild memory issues attributed to normal aging – MMSE 27. Compliant with all prescribed medications.

Language barrier: Khmer. Daughter provides interpretation during visits. Resides alone in a housing commission flat. Cognitive decline obvious – cannot remember medications. Non-adherence to diabetes regimen flagged by endocrinologist.
Speaks English fluently. Lives alone in a penthouse apartment. No cognitive concerns whatsoever. Stopped all medications cold turkey after watching a documentary – now hypertensive crisis.
Patient speaks Farsi; professional interpreter provided. Lives with husband and unmarried son. No dementia, cognitively intact. Takes medications precisely as directed – uses pill organiser.

English is native language. Lives alone in government-subsidised senior housing. Advanced dementia, requires home care workers four times daily. Medications are constantly missed when carers don't supervise.
No English – speaks Croatian. Interpreter arranged through hospital. Resides in a dementia-specific care unit. Severe cognitive impairment, bedbound. All medications given via PEG tube.
Fluent English. Lives alone in a cabin in the mountains. Very independent, no cognitive issues. Reports he occasionally forgets to pack medications when travelling – maybe 3-4 times yearly.
Language: Japanese, minimal English. Phone interpreter used. Lives with daughter and her family. No signs of cognitive impairment. Adherence to medications described as meticulous.

English speaker. Lives alone after partner moved to nursing home. Cognitive assessment shows moderate impairment, probable Alzheimer's. Community nurse finds medications untouched days after filling the dosette box.
Patient speaks only Albanian; interpreter booked. Lives in a shared house with three other elderly residents. No cognitive deficits. Medication administration supervised daily by a care attendant.
No language barrier. Lives with her identical twin sister. Both are cognitively sharp at 90. Adherence is excellent – they check each other's medications.

Language barrier: Swahili. Interpreters used for all medical encounters. Lives alone in a studio apartment. Dementia with Lewy bodies diagnosed last year, hallucinations present. Medications often found in odd places – clearly not taken.
Fluent English. Lives in a monastic community with other nuns. Age-related memory decline but fully functional. Compliant with her simple medication regimen.

Only speaks Burmese; uses hospital translation services. Lives alone in supported accommodation for refugees. No cognitive deficit. Takes medications inconsistently due to misunderstanding instructions – language barrier affecting health literacy.
English is his primary language. Lives with wife and disabled son. Early Parkinson's dementia noted. Wife manages medications, says he is 100% adherent to the schedule she sets.
Speaks Hindi; interpreter provided. Lives alone in a rented room. Fully oriented, no cognitive impairment. Struggles to afford medications, often skips doses to extend supply.
No language barrier. Lives alone after children moved interstate. Very mild cognitive changes – occasionally misplaces items, no functional impact. Takes all medications religiously.
Language: Nepali, no English. Family member interprets. Lives with nephew. Clear cognitive impairment – cannot remember year or president. Family ensures medications are given on time.

English is mother tongue. Has lived alone in the same house for 60 years. Fully independent, no cognitive decline evident. Intentionally stopped taking warfarin because of bruising – did not inform GP.
Patient speaks only Italian; interpreter utilized. Lives in a retirement home with own apartment. Mild cognitive decline, uses memory aids effectively. Medications administered by visiting nurse.
Fluent English. Lives with his grown son and daughter-in-law. No memory problems. Non-adherent to antidepressants – skips doses on weekends when drinking alcohol.
Language barrier: Kurdish. Professional interpreter provided by health service. Lives alone in emergency housing. Cognitive assessment normal. Takes medications sporadically due to chaotic lifestyle.
No language barrier. Lives in a convent with other sisters, communal lifestyle. Diagnosed with Alzheimer's two years ago, progressing slowly. All medications supervised by infirmary staff.
Speaks only Pashto; interpreter essential. Lives alone in a house he built decades ago. Severe dementia, wanders at night. Medications are barely taken – community nurse finds them scattered.
